startScreenOptions.title: AN52.1-AN52.8 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN52.1-AN52.8.

interactions.label: In a neonate with duodenal atresia, the vomiting is bilious (green) because:

params.question: In a neonate with duodenal atresia, the vomiting is bilious (green) because:

answers.text: The obstruction is above the ampulla of Vater, trapping bile in the gallbladder

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: An obstruction above the ampulla would produce non-bilious vomiting, since bile cannot reach the gut lumen proximal to the block.

answers.text: The liver directly secretes bile into the gastric lumen under back-pressure

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The liver secretes bile into the biliary system, not directly into the stomach. Bile reaches the gut only via the common bile duct at the ampulla of Vater.

answers.text: The obstruction is below the ampulla of Vater, allowing bile to mix with contents above the block

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The ampulla of Vater drains bile into the second part of the duodenum. When the obstruction lies distal to this point, bile enters the duodenal lumen and is vomited back.

answers.text: Pancreatic enzymes break down haemoglobin in swallowed blood, producing a green colour

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Green vomiting in neonates indicates bile, not altered blood. Haemoglobin breakdown produces coffee-ground or dark material, not the bright green of bilious vomitus.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the anatomy: the ampulla of Vater drains bile into the second part of the duodenum. If the obstruction is below this point, bile mixes with the trapped contents and is vomited green.
Well done. Bilious vomiting in a neonate is a surgical red flag — the relationship between the obstruction site and the ampulla of Vater determines whether vomitus contains bile.

interactions.label: A testicular biopsy shows seminiferous tubules lined only by Sertoli cells, with

params.question: A testicular biopsy shows seminiferous tubules lined only by Sertoli cells, with no spermatogenic cells. Leydig cells in the interstitium appear normal. What is the expected clinical consequence?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Testosterone is produced by Leydig cells, which are normal in this condition. Serum testosterone levels remain adequate.
Correct. In Sertoli-cell-only syndrome, the absence of spermatogenic cells means no sperm are produced (azoospermia), but Leydig cells continue to secrete testosterone normally.

answers.text: Oligospermia with elevated FSH and low testosterone

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes a mixed testicular failure. In Sertoli-cell-only syndrome, spermatogenic cells are completely absent (azoospermia, not oligospermia), and Leydig cell function is preserved.

answers.text: Normal sperm count but impaired sperm motility

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Without spermatogenic cells, no spermatozoa are produced at all. The defect is in sperm production, not motility.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember the division of labour in the testis: Sertoli cells support spermatogenesis within seminiferous tubules, while Leydig cells in the interstitium produce testosterone. Loss of spermatogenic cells causes azoospermia but does not affect testosterone.
Exactly right. Sertoli-cell-only syndrome causes infertility through azoospermia, while testosterone production by Leydig cells remains unaffected.

interactions.label: In Hirschsprung's disease, the bowel proximal to the aganglionic segment dilates

params.question: In Hirschsprung's disease, the bowel proximal to the aganglionic segment dilates into a megacolon because:

answers.text: The aganglionic segment undergoes excessive uncoordinated peristalsis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Without ganglion cells, the affected segment cannot generate peristalsis at all. The problem is the opposite — the segment remains tonically contracted.

answers.text: Neural crest cells in the proximal bowel become hyperactive, increasing motility

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The proximal bowel is normally innervated. It dilates because of the downstream functional obstruction, not because of its own neural overactivity.

answers.text: The aganglionic segment cannot relax, creating a functional obstruction that traps contents upstream

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Without enteric ganglia, the affected segment stays tonically contracted and cannot undergo the coordinated relaxation needed for peristalsis. Faecal matter accumulates proximally, dilating the normal bowel.

answers.text: The smooth muscle in the affected segment is replaced by fibrous tissue, narrowing the lumen

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The smooth muscle is structurally intact in Hirschsprung's disease. The defect is in the enteric nervous system (absent ganglion cells from failed neural crest migration), not in the muscle itself.

overallFeedback.feedback: In Hirschsprung's disease, neural crest cells fail to colonise the distal colon. The resulting aganglionic segment cannot relax, acting as a functional obstruction. Contents accumulate above it, dilating the normally innervated proximal bowel into a megacolon.
Correct. The key concept is that the aganglionic segment remains tonically contracted — it is a functional obstruction, not a mechanical one, that causes proximal dilatation.

interactions.label: In female development, the absence of Anti-Müllerian Hormone allows the Wolffian

params.question: In female development, the absence of Anti-Müllerian Hormone allows the Wolffian ducts to persist and form the uterine tubes, uterus, and upper vagina.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Look carefully: the statement says 'Wolffian ducts', but these are the male duct system preserved by testosterone. In females, it is the Müllerian ducts that persist when AMH is absent, forming the uterine tubes, uterus, and upper vagina. The Wolffian ducts regress without testosterone.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. It is the Müllerian ducts (not the Wolffian ducts) that persist in females. Without AMH, the Müllerian ducts develop into the uterine tubes, uterus, and upper vagina. The Wolffian ducts regress in the absence of testosterone.
